edges.description: Glycogen-overloaded lysosomes perturb lysosome-dependent autophagic flux in muscle.
Glycogen storage in skeletal and respiratory muscles disrupts contractile tissue and respiratory mechanics.
Glycogen storage in cardiomyocytes drives the hypertrophic cardiac phenotype of infantile-onset disease.
edges.target: Cardiomyocyte glycogen storage and hypertrophic remodeling

edges.description: Lysosomal glycogen storage in infantile-onset disease can involve liver enlargement.
Glycogen storage in tongue musculature contributes to macroglossia in infantile-onset disease.
The broader lysosomal-storage phenotype includes hearing impairment in a minority of Pompe patients.
In late-onset disease, systemic glycogen-storage pathology can include adult arteriopathy with ascending thoracic aortic dilatation.
Autophagic buildup and impaired lysosomal delivery contribute to progressive muscle fiber damage.
Skeletal muscle fiber injury clinically manifests as progressive proximal and limb-girdle weakness.
Respiratory myofiber injury weakens ventilatory muscles and causes respiratory insufficiency.
Infantile Pompe myofiber injury manifests as hypotonia and generalized muscle weakness.
Feeding difficulty and poor growth follow from severe infantile muscle and respiratory involvement.
Muscle weakness and hypotonia delay acquisition of motor milestones in infantile Pompe disease.
Ongoing skeletal muscle injury releases creatine kinase into circulation.
Progressive limb-girdle and respiratory myofiber injury limits sustained activity.
Progressive skeletal and respiratory muscle involvement contributes to chronic fatigue.
Oropharyngeal and esophageal muscle weakness can impair swallowing.
Axial and paraspinal muscle weakness contributes to scoliosis.
Respiratory muscle weakness and impaired airway clearance predispose to recurrent respiratory infections.
Cardiomyocyte glycogen storage and hypertrophic remodeling produce the infantile-onset hypertrophic cardiomyopathy phenotype.
edges.source: Cardiomyocyte glycogen storage and hypertrophic remodeling

edges.description: Glycogen deposition in cardiac conduction tissue causes conduction defects with shortened PR interval in infantile-onset Pompe disease.
edges.source: Cardiomyocyte glycogen storage and hypertrophic remodeling

edges.description: Decreased acid alpha-glucosidase activity reports the upstream enzymatic defect that permits lysosomal glycogen storage.
Elevated urinary Glc4 reports increased glycogen turnover from lysosomal glycogen storage.

nodes.description: Beyond glycogen accumulation, Pompe disease involves profound dysregulation of autophagy. Autophagic buildup in type II muscle fibers represents a major pathological feature in both IOPD and LOPD. Impaired autophagosome-lysosome fusion leads to accumulation of autophagic debris, which can be as destructive to muscle architecture as glycogen storage itself. This autophagic pathology may limit the effectiveness of enzyme replacement therapy.

evidence.reference_title: Autophagy in skeletal muscle: implications for Pompe disease.
evidence.snippet: In the GAA-knockout mouse model, progressive accumulation of autophagic vesicles is restricted to Type II-rich muscle fibers. Not only does this build-up of autophagosomes disrupt the contractile apparatus in the muscle fibers, it also interferes with enzyme replacement therapy by acting as a sink for the recombinant enzyme and preventing its efficient delivery to the lysosomes.

evidence.reference_title: Failure of Autophagy in Pompe Disease.
evidence.snippet: The autophagic pathway is largely responsible for the delivery of cytosolic glycogen to the lysosome where it is degraded to glucose via acid α-glucosidase.

nodes.description: Cardiac medication and anesthesia planning require Pompe-specific caution. GeneReviews warns that digoxin, inotropes, diuretics, and afterload-reducing agents may worsen left ventricular outflow obstruction in some stages, and that anesthesia should be used only when necessary because cardiovascular and respiratory reserve may be limited.

evidence.reference_title: Pompe Disease.
evidence.snippet: The use of digoxin, ionotropes, diuretics, and afterload-reducing agents may worsen left ventricular outflow obstruction

evidence.reference_title: Pompe Disease.
evidence.snippet: Anesthesia should be used only when absolutely necessary because reduced cardiovascular return and underlying respiratory insufficiency pose significant risks.

nodes.description: In infantile-onset Pompe disease, cardiomyocyte glycogen storage produces cardiac hypertrophy and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy that contribute to early cardiorespiratory morbidity when untreated.
nodes.id: Cardiomyocyte glycogen storage and hypertrophic remodeling

evidence.reference_title: Pompe's disease.
evidence.snippet: Cardiac hypertrophy is a key feature of classic infantile Pompe's disease.

evidence.reference_title: Recombinant human acid [alpha]-glucosidase: major clinical benefits in infantile-onset Pompe disease.
evidence.snippet: Infantile-onset Pompe disease is characterized by cardiomyopathy, respiratory and skeletal muscle weakness, and early death.

nodes.description: A two-component therapy combining cipaglucosidase alfa, a novel recombinant human acid alpha-glucosidase, with miglustat, an enzyme stabilizer that enhances intracellular delivery. FDA-approved in 2023 for adults with late-onset Pompe disease. The PROPEL phase 3 trial (125 patients) showed numerical improvements in 6-minute walk distance and respiratory function versus alglucosidase alfa, with benefits particularly in patients previously receiving ERT for more than 2 years.

evidence.reference_title: Safety and efficacy of cipaglucosidase alfa plus miglustat versus alglucosidase alfa plus placebo in late-onset Pompe disease (PROPEL): an international, randomised, double-blind, parallel-group, phase 3 trial.
evidence.snippet: At week 52, mean change from baseline in 6-min walk distance was 20·8 m (SE 4·6) in the cipaglucosidase alfa plus miglustat group versus 7·2 m (6·6) in the alglucosidase alfa plus placebo group using last observation carried forward (between-group difference 13·6 m [95% CI -2·8 to 29·9]).

evidence.reference_title: Advances in diagnosis and management of Pompe disease.
evidence.snippet: The diagnosis requires demonstration of deficiency of the lysosomal acid alpha-glucosidase enzyme, which can be assayed in dried blood spot or liquid blood samples, together with supportive biomarker tests, and confirmed with molecular genetic analysis.

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen storage disease due to acid maltase deficiency (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0034932 | Decreased circulating acid maltase activity | Very frequent (99-80%)

readouts.interpretation: Decreased acid alpha-glucosidase activity reports the upstream enzymatic defect that permits lysosomal glycogen storage.

nodes.description: High-protein diet may help preserve muscle mass. In IOPD, specialized feeding support including nasogastric or gastrostomy feeding may be needed.

evidence.reference_title: Exercise training alone or in combination with high-protein diet in patients with late onset Pompe disease: results of a cross over study.
evidence.snippet: Exercise training, particularly if combined with high-protein diet, could reverse this decrease and result in an improvement, which was accompanied by improved quality of life.

nodes.description: Swallowing difficulties may occur in Pompe disease due to weakness of oropharyngeal and esophageal musculature.

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen storage disease due to acid maltase deficiency (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0002015 | Dysphagia | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Serum creatine kinase (CK) levels are typically elevated in Pompe disease, reflecting ongoing muscle damage. CK levels are generally 2-15 times the upper limit of normal.

evidence.reference_title: Infantile Pompe disease: A case report and review of the Chinese literature.
evidence.snippet: cardiac hypertrophy, hepatomegaly and elevated serum enzyme levels, which was characterized by an aspartate aminotransferase level of 95 U/l, lactate dehydrogenase level of 778 U/l and creatine kinase level of 1,299 U/l

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen storage disease due to acid maltase deficiency (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0003236 | Elevated circulating creatine kinase concentration | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Intravenous infusion of recombinant human GAA is the standard of care for Pompe disease. Alglucosidase alfa (Myozyme/Lumizyme) was the first approved ERT (2006). Avalglucosidase alfa (Nexviazyme), a next-generation ERT with enhanced mannose-6-phosphate receptor binding, was approved in 2021 and shows improved skeletal muscle uptake. ERT significantly improves survival and cardiac outcomes in IOPD and stabilizes or improves respiratory and motor function in LOPD.

evidence.reference_title: Recombinant human acid [alpha]-glucosidase: major clinical benefits in infantile-onset Pompe disease.
evidence.snippet: All patients (100%) survived to 18 months of age. A Cox proportional hazards analysis demonstrated that treatment reduced the risk of death by 99%, reduced the risk of death or invasive ventilation by 92%, and reduced the risk of death or any type of ventilation by 88%, as compared to an untreated historical control group.

evidence.reference_title: A randomized study of alglucosidase alfa in late-onset Pompe's disease.
evidence.snippet: At 78 weeks, the estimated mean changes from baseline in the primary end points favored alglucosidase alfa (an increase of 28.1+/-13.1 m on the 6-minute walk test and an absolute increase of 3.4+/-1.2 percentage points in FVC; P=0.03 and P=0.006, respectively).

evidence.reference_title: Safety and efficacy of avalglucosidase alfa versus alglucosidase alfa in patients with late-onset Pompe disease (COMET): a phase 3, randomised, multicentre trial.
evidence.snippet: We consider that this study provides evidence of clinically meaningful improvement with avalglucosidase alfa therapy over alglucosidase alfa in respiratory function, ambulation, and functional endurance, with no new safety signals reported.

nodes.description: Reduced ability to sustain physical activity is a common feature of Pompe disease, particularly in LOPD, reflecting progressive skeletal muscle glycogen accumulation and energy metabolism impairment.

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen storage disease due to acid maltase deficiency (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0003546 | Exercise intolerance | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Poor weight gain and growth failure in IOPD due to feeding difficulties and systemic energy deficit from impaired glycogen metabolism.

evidence.reference_title: A retrospective, multinational, multicenter study on the natural history of infantile-onset Pompe disease.
evidence.snippet: feeding difficulties (57%), and failure to thrive (53%) appeared after a median age of approximately 4.0 months

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen storage disease due to acid maltase deficiency (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0001508 | Failure to thrive | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Chronic fatigue is a commonly reported symptom in Pompe disease, affecting quality of life in both IOPD and LOPD patients.

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen storage disease due to acid maltase deficiency (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0012378 | Fatigue | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.reference_title: Pompe disease: pathogenesis, molecular genetics and diagnosis.
evidence.snippet: Currently, more than 560 mutations spread throughout GAA gene have been reported.

evidence.reference_title: GAA / glycogen storage disease II (Definitive)
evidence.snippet: GAA | HGNC:4065 | glycogen storage disease II | MONDO:0009290 | AR | Definitive

nodes.description: Severe generalized hypotonia (floppy infant) is a cardinal feature of IOPD. Affected infants show profound muscle weakness and poor head control. In LOPD, hypotonia may be less severe but progressive.

evidence.reference_title: Advances in diagnosis and management of Pompe disease.
evidence.snippet: Pompe disease manifests with a broad spectrum of disease severity, ranging from severe infantile-onset diseases such as hypotonia and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy to late-onset diseases such as myopathy and respiratory compromise.

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen storage disease due to acid maltase deficiency (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0008947 | Floppy infant | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Genetic counseling for families with Pompe disease addresses autosomal recessive recurrence risk, carrier testing for relatives, and prenatal or preimplantation genetic testing once familial GAA variants are known.

evidence.reference_title: Pompe Disease.
evidence.snippet: Pompe disease is inherited in an autosomal recessive manner.

nodes.description: Sensorineural hearing loss has been reported in Pompe disease, likely related to glycogen accumulation in the inner ear structures.

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen storage disease due to acid maltase deficiency (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0000365 | Hearing impairment | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Liver enlargement due to glycogen accumulation is common in IOPD but uncommon in LOPD.

evidence.reference_title: Infantile Pompe disease: A case report and review of the Chinese literature.
evidence.snippet: a newborn who was found to have cardiac hypertrophy, hepatomegaly and elevated serum enzyme levels

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen storage disease due to acid maltase deficiency (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0002240 | Hepatomegaly | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Massive cardiac glycogen storage causes biventricular hypertrophic cardiomyopathy in infantile-onset Pompe disease. Left ventricular wall thickness may be dramatically increased. This is the hallmark feature distinguishing IOPD from LOPD and is a major cause of death if untreated.

evidence.reference_title: Pompe's disease.
evidence.snippet: Cardiac hypertrophy is a key feature of classic infantile Pompe's disease.

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen storage disease due to acid maltase deficiency (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0001639 | Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy | Occasional (29-5%)

evidence.reference_title: Recombinant human acid [alpha]-glucosidase: major clinical benefits in infantile-onset Pompe disease.
evidence.snippet: Infantile-onset Pompe disease is characterized by cardiomyopathy, respiratory and skeletal muscle weakness, and early death.

nodes.description: Deficiency of acid alpha-glucosidase (GAA) prevents normal lysosomal degradation of glycogen. Glycogen progressively accumulates within lysosomes, establishing the substrate-storage lesion that drives downstream autophagic, skeletal muscle, respiratory muscle, and cardiac pathology.

evidence.reference_title: Pompe disease: pathogenesis, molecular genetics and diagnosis.
evidence.snippet: GAA catalyzes the hydrolysis of α-1,4 and α-1,6-glucosidic bonds of glycogen and its deficiency leads to lysosomal storage of glycogen in several tissues, particularly in muscle.

evidence.reference_title: Pompe's disease.
evidence.snippet: acid alpha-glucosidase deficiency leading to lysosomal glycogen storage

nodes.description: Enlargement of the tongue is a characteristic feature of IOPD, caused by glycogen accumulation in the tongue musculature.

evidence.reference_title: Enzyme replacement therapy and immunotherapy lead to significant functional improvement in two children with Pompe disease: a case report.
evidence.snippet: presented with generalized hypotonia, macroglossia, hyporeflexia, and mild left ventricular hypertrophy

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen storage disease due to acid maltase deficiency (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0000158 | Macroglossia | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Delayed motor milestones in IOPD; infants often never achieve independent sitting or walking without treatment. In LOPD, motor decline may manifest as loss of previously acquired motor skills.

evidence.reference_title: Enzyme replacement therapy and immunotherapy lead to significant functional improvement in two children with Pompe disease: a case report.
evidence.snippet: Functional tests revealed motor development delay, generalized hypotonia, and positive anti-recombinant human acid alpha-glucosidase IgG antibody titers

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen storage disease due to acid maltase deficiency (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0001270 | Motor delay | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Structured physical therapy and rehabilitation programs help maintain mobility, prevent contractures, and optimize residual muscle function. Exercise programs should be tailored to avoid overexertion.

evidence.reference_title: Adapted physical activity and therapeutic exercise in late-onset Pompe disease (LOPD): a two-step rehabilitative approach.
evidence.snippet: Therefore, we evaluated the role of physical activity, therapeutic exercise, and pulmonary rehabilitation programs in order to promote an appropriate management of motor and respiratory dysfunctions and improve QoL in patients with LOPD.

nodes.description: Progressive weakness of proximal and limb-girdle muscles is the predominant feature of LOPD. Patients develop difficulty climbing stairs, rising from chairs, and walking. The pelvic girdle is typically affected before the shoulder girdle.

evidence.reference_title: Pompe disease: pathogenesis, molecular genetics and diagnosis.
evidence.snippet: PD is a chronic and progressive pathology usually characterized by limb-girdle muscle weakness and respiratory failure.

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen storage disease due to acid maltase deficiency (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0009073 | Progressive proximal muscle weakness | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Recurrent respiratory infections are a frequent complication of Pompe disease, resulting from respiratory muscle weakness and impaired airway clearance.

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen storage disease due to acid maltase deficiency (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0002205 | Recurrent respiratory infections | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Diaphragmatic and respiratory muscle weakness leads to progressive respiratory insufficiency. In LOPD, respiratory failure may be the presenting symptom and can precede limb weakness. Many patients eventually require ventilatory support. In IOPD, respiratory failure contributes to early death.

evidence.reference_title: A randomized study of alglucosidase alfa in late-onset Pompe's disease.
evidence.snippet: Late-onset Pompe's disease is characterized by progressive muscle weakness and loss of respiratory function, leading to early death.

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen storage disease due to acid maltase deficiency (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0002747 | Respiratory insufficiency due to muscle weakness | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Non-invasive ventilation (BiPAP) or invasive mechanical ventilation may be required for respiratory insufficiency. Many LOPD patients require nocturnal ventilatory support, and some progress to continuous ventilator dependence.

evidence.reference_title: Consensus treatment recommendations for late-onset Pompe disease.
evidence.snippet: A multidisciplinary team should be involved to properly treat the pulmonary, neuromuscular, orthopedic, and gastrointestinal elements of late-onset Pompe disease.

evidence.reference_title: Effectiveness of Respiratory Muscle Training in Pompe Disease: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
evidence.snippet: RMT has potential to be integrated into the cardioplulmonary rehabilitation for patients with Pompe disease.

nodes.description: Spinal deformity secondary to paraspinal muscle weakness, occurring particularly in patients with progressive proximal myopathy.

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen storage disease due to acid maltase deficiency (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0002650 | Scoliosis | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Glycogen deposition in cardiac conduction tissue produces conduction defects with shortening of the PR interval on EKG in infantile-onset Pompe disease.

evidence.reference_title: Pompe Disease.
evidence.snippet: Progressive deposition of glycogen results in conduction defects with shortening of the PR interval on EKG.

nodes.description: Glycogen storage and autophagic debris injure skeletal and respiratory muscle fibers, producing progressive limb-girdle weakness, exercise intolerance, and respiratory insufficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Pompe disease: pathogenesis, molecular genetics and diagnosis.
evidence.snippet: PD is a chronic and progressive pathology usually characterized by limb-girdle muscle weakness and respiratory failure.

evidence.reference_title: Exercise training alone or in combination with high-protein diet in patients with late onset Pompe disease: results of a cross over study.
evidence.snippet: The GAA deficiency results into glycogen accumulation, especially in fibers of skeletal, respiratory and cardiac muscles; the consequence is a progressive invalidating disease

nodes.description: Adults with late-onset Pompe disease can develop arteriopathy including dilatation of the ascending thoracic aorta, prompting echocardiographic and vascular surveillance.

evidence.reference_title: Pompe Disease.
evidence.snippet: Some adults have developed arteriopathy, including dilatation of the ascending thoracic aorta.

evidence.reference_title: Liquid chromatographic assay for a glucose tetrasaccharide, a putative biomarker for the diagnosis of Pompe disease.
evidence.snippet: all the patients had elevated Glc(4) levels compared with age-matched controls.

evidence.reference_title: Pompe Disease.
evidence.snippet: CK level, and urine total glucotetrasaccharide (Hex4) level.

readouts.interpretation: Elevated urinary Glc4 reports increased glycogen turnover from lysosomal glycogen storage.

nodes.description: The most common GAA pathogenic variant in Caucasian populations. This intronic variant affects splicing efficiency, allowing some normal transcript production and residual enzyme activity. Typically associated with late-onset disease when compound heterozygous with another pathogenic variant.
